title: Why the trigger of being asked to close your eyes feels dangerous
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your body knows something your mind forgot

I was in a yoga class. The instructor said the words I've heard a thousand times: "Close your eyes and turn your attention inward." And my body went cold. Not metaphorically cold. Actually cold. My hands started shaking. My throat tightened. I wanted to run. Right there on my mat, in a room full of people breathing calmly, I wanted to flee.

I didn't understand it then. I thought I was just bad at relaxation. Bad at meditation. Bad at being present. I thought there was something wrong with me because I couldn't do what everyone else seemed to do so easily. Here's the thing: there wasn't something wrong with me. My nervous system was telling the truth. And your nervous system might be telling you the same thing right now.

When someone tells you to close your eyes, they're asking you to surrender a primary survival tool. Your eyes scan for threat. They track movement. They gauge distance. They read faces. For those of us who grew up in unpredictable or dangerous environments, keeping our eyes open was not a preference. It was a strategy. It kept us alive.

So when a teacher, a therapist, or a well-meaning friend says "just close your eyes and relax," your body doesn't hear "relax." Your body hears "drop your defenses in a room full of potential danger." And it says no. Loudly. Physically.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals and humans process threat. He noticed something crucial: immobilization is a trauma response. When prey animals freeze, they often go still and shut down their sensory input. Closing your eyes can trigger that same ancient freeze response. Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. Stay with me here.

I'm not saying everyone who hates closing their eyes has trauma. But if your body reacts with anxiety, dread, or dissociation when asked to shut your eyes, it's worth asking why. That reaction isn't weakness. It's wisdom. Your nervous system is telling you something important about what it needed to survive.

The vigilance that kept you safe

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called "neuroception." It's your nervous system's ability to detect safety and danger without conscious thought. Your body is scanning constantly. Every sound. Every shift in the air. Every person's posture. And when you close your eyes, you cut off a major channel of that scanning. For a traumatized nervous system, that feels like flying blind.

Think about it. If you grew up with a parent whose mood could shift without warning, you learned to watch. You learned to read the subtle signs. The tightening of a jaw. The change in breathing. The slight shift in posture. Those micro-signals told you whether it was safe to speak, to move, to exist. Closing your eyes would have been dangerous. You needed to see the threat coming.

That vigilance didn't disappear when you left that environment. It became a permanent feature of your nervous system. And now, when someone asks you to close your eyes, your body activates the same protective response it used when you were small. It's not being difficult. It's being faithful to its job.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker writes extensively about the "outer critic" and the hypervigilance that trauma survivors carry. He names what many of us have felt but couldn't articulate: the constant scanning, the inability to relax, the feeling that danger is always around the corner. Reading it can feel like someone finally understands what your body has been trying to say.

I've worked with clients who describe closing their eyes as "falling into a void." They feel untethered. Disoriented. Like they might disappear. That's not dramatic. That's a trauma response. When your sense of safety depends on staying alert, letting go of that alertness can feel like letting go of yourself.

What your therapist probably missed

Here's where it gets complicated. Many therapists, yoga teachers, and meditation guides are trained to encourage eye-closing as part of relaxation or mindfulness practice. They mean well. They truly do. But they often don't understand what they're asking of a traumatized nervous system. And when you can't do it, when you keep your eyes open or feel panicked, you might get labeled as resistant. Difficult. Not ready for healing.

That's not just unhelpful. It's re-traumatizing. It tells your nervous system that its protective response is wrong. That your body is lying to you. That you should override your instincts and do what you're told. Sound familiar? For many of us, that dynamic echoes the original trauma. The message was: your feelings don't matter. Your body doesn't matter. Do what you're told and don't make a fuss.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma-related dissociation and structural dissociation, emphasizes that the parts of us that resist or protect are not problems to be eliminated. They are survival strategies that kept us alive. When you feel that urge to open your eyes, that's not a failure. That's a part of you doing its job. Honoring that part is where real healing begins.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work helps explain why different parts of you have different responses to the same trigger. The part that wants to close your eyes and relax is real. So is the part that screams no. Both are valid. Both need to be heard.

I've learned to modify my own practice. I don't close my eyes in meditation anymore. I keep them softly focused on a spot on the floor. Or I meditate with my eyes open, looking at a candle or a tree outside the window. Some days I close them for three seconds and then open them again. That's not failure. That's working with my nervous system instead of against it.

Wild, right? The thing that's supposed to help you relax actually triggers your survival response. And the solution isn't to force yourself through it. The solution is to listen to what your body is saying and find a different way.

How to work with this trigger instead of fighting it

So what do you do if closing your eyes feels dangerous? First, stop trying to force it. Your body's resistance is not a character flaw. It's information. Treat it like a friend who's trying to warn you about something. That friend might be overreacting. But they're not wrong to care.

Start by experimenting. Can you close your eyes for one second? Two seconds? Can you close them with your back against a wall? With someone you trust in the room? With your hand on your chest? The goal isn't to achieve eye-closing. The goal is to find the conditions where your nervous system might, gradually, begin to feel safe enough to try.

Here's a practice that helps me: I close my eyes but keep my hands in a position where I can see them through my eyelids. Or I keep one eye slightly open. Or I put my hand on my heart and remind myself: "You are in a safe place. You are an adult now. You can open your eyes at any time." That last part is crucial. Your nervous system needs to know that escape is always available. Always.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb describes how emotional neglect in childhood creates adults who feel disconnected from their own emotions and bodies. If you grew up being told your feelings didn't matter, of course closing your eyes and turning inward feels dangerous. You learned that what's inside you isn't safe to feel.

Another approach: try orienting. This is a technique from Somatic Experiencing. Before you close your eyes, look around the room. Notice the colors. The shapes. The objects. Let your eyes land on something that feels neutral or pleasant. A plant. A book. A window. Then, when you close your eyes, hold that image in your mind. You're giving your nervous system a reference point. A place to return to. It's like leaving a light on for yourself.

I also want to name something that doesn't get talked about enough. For some trauma survivors, closing your eyes triggers dissociation. You don't just feel anxious. You feel like you're leaving your body. Floating away. Becoming unreal. That's a different kind of danger. Your nervous system is saying: if we shut down our external scanning, we might lose ourselves entirely. And it's right to be afraid of that.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helps explain why you learned to suppress your own needs and feelings. If your parents couldn't handle your emotions, you learned to hide them. Closing your eyes and feeling what's inside can feel like opening a door you were told to keep locked.

The path forward is slow. It's not linear. Some days you'll be able to close your eyes for five minutes. Other days you'll make it two seconds before your body says no. Both are wins. Both are your nervous system learning, at its own pace, that it might be safe to let down its guard. Just a little. Just for now.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that last sentence. Did your shoulders drop? Did they tighten? That's your nervous system talking. Keep listening. It's been trying to tell you something for a very long time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
